$comment: StellarLight DATA-LAYER augmentation set for the cf-flue golden-question eval. These 21 questions are data-discriminating: each one can only be answered correctly by querying the live StellarLight tools (records via search_projects, code via search_repos, funding via get_rfps, hackathons via get_hackathon, ecosystem clusters via get_clusters, builders via get_builders, research/docs/audits/SEPs via search_research) — a guesser working from general crypto/LLM knowledge gets them wrong (e.g. assumes RWA treasury issuers are SCF-funded, name-drops Aave/Chainlink instead of the real Stellar records, mis-orders hackathon placements, or calls a Draft SEP final). It is complementary to the protocol-knowledge cf-flue set, which tests conceptual Stellar/Soroban understanding; this set tests grounded what-exists / who-built / what-got-funded data. Every question is liveSource:true. The answerRegex fields are cheap presence ANCHORS for the harness, NOT the grader — the semantic block (intent / mustInclude / mustAvoid / passIf / failIf / inconclusiveIf / canonicalFacts / discriminator) is the source of truth for scoring. Adapter note: most questions use tools already in Tyler's base StellarLight registry, but two tools may need wiring into the cf-flue adapter — search_repos (used by the three code-data questions) and get_hackathon (singular per-event lookup, used by the three hackathon-data questions); these are distinct from the registry's get_hackathons (list) and compare_hackathons. All canonical facts verified against the live API on 2026-06-22.

questions.question: I'm scoping a new money-market / lending protocol on Soroban. Has anyone already built lending and borrowing on Stellar, and which of those existing protocols actually got SCF funding (so I know who the serious, backed teams are)?

semantic.intent: Establish that lending/borrowing is already a crowded, well-funded vertical on Stellar (not greenfield), name Blend as the flagship/base lending primitive, and identify the SCF-funded flagships from the StellarLight directory.
mustInclude: States lending is already heavily built on Stellar (not greenfield).
Names Blend as the flagship / base lending primitive other Stellar DeFi apps build on.
Identifies at least 2 of Slender, DeFindex, OrbitCDP as SCF-funded flagships.
mustAvoid: Do not claim no one has built lending on Stellar.
Do not invent protocol names not in the directory.
Do not list only generic EVM protocols (Aave/Compound) instead of the real Stellar records.
niceToHave: Notes Slender (by eq-lab) received ~$296,750 SCF.
Notes OrbitCDP is a CDP protocol (~$280k SCF) and DeFindex (PaltaLabs) is tokenized-vault yield infra (~$150k SCF).
Mentions additional SCF-funded lending projects such as Laina, Alula, Lumen Later, YieldBack.Cash, Lantern, HedgePay.
Notes unfunded ones like AssetDesk and XycLoans.
passIf: Answer states lending is already heavily built (not greenfield) AND names Blend as the flagship/base lending primitive AND identifies at least 2 of Slender, DeFindex, OrbitCDP as SCF-funded.
failIf: Claims no one has built lending on Stellar.
Invents protocol names not in the directory.
Lists only generic EVM protocols (Aave/Compound) instead of the real Stellar records.
inconclusiveIf: Names Blend and some lending protocols but does not establish which are SCF-funded.
canonicalFacts: The flagship SCF-funded lending protocols are Blend, Slender, DeFindex, and OrbitCDP (all scfAwarded=true, status Live).
Blend is the base lending/yield primitive other Stellar DeFi apps build on.
Slender (by eq-lab) received ~$296,750 SCF; OrbitCDP is a CDP protocol (~$280k SCF); DeFindex (PaltaLabs) is tokenized-vault yield infra (~$150k SCF).
Many more SCF-funded lending projects exist (Laina, Alula, Lumen Later, YieldBack.Cash, Lantern, HedgePay), plus unfunded ones like AssetDesk and XycLoans.
semantic.discriminator: A guesser claims lending is greenfield or name-drops EVM protocols (Aave/Compound); naming Blend as the base primitive plus the real SCF-funded Stellar flagships (Slender/DeFindex/OrbitCDP) with their funded status proves a real StellarLight query.

questions.question: Before I build a price oracle for Soroban, who already provides on-chain price feeds on Stellar, and which of them is the most established / highest-signal one?

semantic.intent: Identify that Stellar already has multiple live oracles (well-covered prior art), name Reflector as the most established / highest-signal one, and name the other SCF-funded oracle flagships from the StellarLight directory.
mustInclude: Identifies Reflector as the leading / most-established Stellar oracle.
Names at least 2 of the other SCF-funded oracles: DIA, Band, Lightecho.
mustAvoid: Do not return no oracle.
Do not name only off-platform oracles (e.g. only Chainlink) without the Stellar-native flagships.
Do not invent an oracle name absent from the directory.
niceToHave: Notes Reflector is a decentralized price-oracle and data-feed network, prominence 90, ~$444,840 SCF awarded (the largest oracle grant).
Notes Reflector powers DeFi protocols including Blend, Laina, and Slender.
Notes DIA (~$38k), Band (~$100k), Lightecho (~$93,110, by BP Ventures, SEP-40 contract for emerging-market FX feeds).
Notes DIA and Band are two of the three oracle providers in the official Stellar Docs.
Mentions additional non-SCF oracles (Redstone, Pyth, Orally, Chainlink relayer).
passIf: Answer identifies Reflector as the leading/most-established Stellar oracle AND names at least 2 of DIA, Band, Lightecho.
failIf: Returns no oracle.
Names only off-platform oracles (e.g. only Chainlink) without the Stellar-native flagships.
Invents an oracle name absent from the directory.
inconclusiveIf: Names Reflector but fails to name at least 2 of the other SCF-funded oracles (DIA, Band, Lightecho).
canonicalFacts: Reflector is the most established / highest-signal Stellar oracle — a decentralized price-oracle and data-feed network, prominence 90, ~$444,840 SCF awarded (the largest oracle grant).
Reflector powers DeFi protocols including Blend, Laina, and Slender.
The other SCF-funded oracle flagships are DIA (~$38k), Band (~$100k), and Lightecho (~$93,110, by BP Ventures, SEP-40 contract for emerging-market FX feeds).
DIA and Band are noted as two of the three oracle providers in the official Stellar Docs; additional non-SCF oracles exist (Redstone, Pyth, Orally, Chainlink relayer).
semantic.discriminator: A guesser names only off-platform oracles like Chainlink; naming Reflector as the leader plus the real SCF-funded Stellar oracles (DIA/Band/Lightecho) proves a real StellarLight query against the directory.

questions.question: I want to tokenize US Treasuries / money-market funds (RWA) on Stellar. Who has already done this, and are these tokenized-treasury projects SCF grant-funded or are they independent institutional issuers?

semantic.intent: Establish that tokenized treasuries on Stellar already exist and are dominated by independent institutional / TradFi issuers (Benji, Ondo, WisdomTree, Spiko), correctly stating these specific flagships are NOT SCF-funded (scfAwarded=false).
mustInclude: Names at least 2 of Benji/Franklin Templeton, Ondo/USDY, WisdomTree, Spiko as existing tokenized-treasury RWA projects.
Correctly states they are NOT SCF-funded / are independent institutional issuers (scfAwarded=false).
mustAvoid: Do not claim no one has tokenized treasuries on Stellar.
Do not assert Benji/Ondo/WisdomTree/Spiko are SCF grant-funded.
Do not fabricate issuer names not in the directory.
niceToHave: Notes Benji is Franklin Templeton's FOBXX, the first U.S.-registered mutual fund on a public blockchain.
Notes Ondo is USDY, a yield-bearing token backed by short-term US Treasuries.
Notes WisdomTree is WisdomTree Prime (incl. WTGXX treasury fund) and Spiko runs regulated EUTBL/USTBL T-bill funds.
Notes SCF money in the broader RWA space goes to tooling/infra like Spydra, Dobprotocol, Legasi, Neovestor — not the headline treasury issuers.
passIf: Answer names at least 2 of {Benji/Franklin Templeton, Ondo/USDY, WisdomTree, Spiko} AND correctly states they are NOT SCF-funded / are institutional issuers (scfAwarded=false).
failIf: Claims no one has tokenized treasuries on Stellar.
Incorrectly asserts Benji/Ondo/WisdomTree/Spiko are SCF grant-funded.
Fabricates issuer names not in the directory.
inconclusiveIf: Names the issuers but is unclear or silent on whether they are SCF-funded vs independent.
canonicalFacts: Tokenized treasuries on Stellar are dominated by independent institutional / TradFi issuers, NOT SCF grantees.

The flagship records — Benji (Franklin Templeton's FOBXX, the first U.S.-registered mutual fund on a public blockchain), Ondo (USDY, yield-bearing token backed by short-term US Treasuries), WisdomTree (WisdomTree Prime, incl. WTGXX treasury fund), and Spiko (regulated EUTBL/USTBL T-bill funds) — are ALL scfAwarded=false.
SCF money in the broader RWA space goes to tooling/infra like Spydra, Dobprotocol, Legasi, Neovestor — not the headline treasury issuers.
semantic.discriminator: An agent guessing would assume the big-name RWA projects were grant-funded; the data refutes this (all scfAwarded=false), so correctly stating they are independent institutional issuers proves a real StellarLight query rather than a guess.

questions.question: I'm building a passkey / smart-wallet flow on Stellar and want to start from the strongest existing open-source code. Which indexed repo is the highest-quality reference for a passkey wallet, and what are the other Stellar-org repos worth looking at?

semantic.intent: Identify the highest-repoScore indexed repo for a passkey wallet on Stellar (stellar/typescript-wallet-sdk) and surface the other verified Stellar-org references, using real owner/name repo identifiers rather than a generic passkey explainer.
mustInclude: Names stellar/typescript-wallet-sdk as the top/highest-repoScore reference (repoScore 56).
Names at least one other repo from the verified set: stellar/wallet-backend (42), stellar/smart-wallet-demo-app (41), or nathanofzion/zi-playground (40).
mustAvoid: Do not invent a repo full-name not in the verified set.
Do not omit stellar/typescript-wallet-sdk as the leader.
Do not answer with only a generic 'use passkeys' explanation with no real repo full-name.
niceToHave: Notes nathanofzion/zi-playground is the SCF-awarded community repo (40), a passkey-ID smart wallet.
Gives full owner/name repo identifiers for each.
Leads with typescript-wallet-sdk as the highest-scored rather than just name-dropping a wallet.
passIf: Names stellar/typescript-wallet-sdk as the top/highest-repoScore reference AND at least one other repo from the verified set (stellar/wallet-backend, stellar/smart-wallet-demo-app, or nathanofzion/zi-playground), with full owner/name repo identifiers.
failIf: Invents a repo not in the set.
Omits typescript-wallet-sdk as the leader.
Answers with only a generic 'use passkeys' explanation with no real repo full-name.
inconclusiveIf: Names typescript-wallet-sdk but is unclear whether it is the highest-scored reference, or gives no second repo.
canonicalFacts: Top reference by repoScore is stellar/typescript-wallet-sdk (repoScore 56).
Other strong Stellar-org references: stellar/wallet-backend (42), stellar/smart-wallet-demo-app (41), and the SCF-awarded community repo nathanofzion/zi-playground (40, a passkey-ID smart wallet).
semantic.discriminator: A guesser gives a generic 'use passkeys' answer or name-drops a wallet without the ranked repo full-names; surfacing typescript-wallet-sdk (repoScore 56) as the leader plus a second verified repo proves a real search_repos query.

questions.question: What's the best zero-knowledge (ZK) reference implementation on Stellar Soroban right now, and did it actually win a hackathon? Give me the top two ZK repos by quality score.

semantic.intent: Identify the top ZK repo on Stellar Soroban by repoScore (fredericrezeau/xray-games, 84) including that it won Stellar Hacks: ZK Gaming, and name the second (Shreshtthh/Zk-Seep, 70), with real repo full-names rather than a generic Noir/Circom answer.
mustInclude: Names fredericrezeau/xray-games as the #1 ZK repo with repoScore 84 ('high').
States it won a hackathon (Stellar Hacks: ZK Gaming).
Names Shreshtthh/Zk-Seep as the second ZK repo (repoScore 70).
mustAvoid: Do not claim a non-seed repo is the top ZK reference.
Do not get the hackathon-winner flag wrong (xray-games did win).
Do not give only a generic 'use Noir/Circom' answer with no repo full-names.
niceToHave: Describes xray-games as a trustless ZK gaming arcade with Soroban contracts + Circom/Noir circuits (judgeScore 0.99).
Notes Zk-Seep is a Noir-based ZK card game on Soroban from the same ZK Gaming hackathon (judgeScore 0.81).
Reports both repoScores as 'high'.
passIf: Names fredericrezeau/xray-games as the #1 ZK repo with its high repoScore (84) AND correctly states it won a hackathon (Stellar Hacks: ZK Gaming), AND names Shreshtthh/Zk-Seep as the second.
failIf: Claims a non-seed repo is the top ZK reference.
Gets the winner flag wrong.
Gives only a generic 'use Noir/Circom' answer with no repo full-names.
inconclusiveIf: Names xray-games as top with the win but omits or is unsure about the second repo (Zk-Seep).
canonicalFacts: Top ZK repo is fredericrezeau/xray-games (repoScore 84, 'high') — a trustless ZK gaming arcade with Soroban contracts + Circom/Noir circuits — and it won Stellar Hacks: ZK Gaming (judgeScore 0.99).
Second is Shreshtthh/Zk-Seep (repoScore 70, 'high'), a Noir-based ZK card game on Soroban from the same ZK Gaming hackathon (judgeScore 0.81).
semantic.discriminator: A guesser gives a generic 'use Noir/Circom' answer or invents a top repo; naming xray-games (84) as the winner of Stellar Hacks: ZK Gaming plus Zk-Seep (70) as #2 proves a real search_repos query with the hackathon-winner join.

questions.question: I want to study a Soroban oracle / price-feed contract before building my own. The three repos that show up for 'soroban oracle' all look low-quality — which is the least bad to start from, and is there anything better-scored I should look at instead?

semantic.intent: Rank the three low-score 'soroban oracle' seed repos by repoScore (feeders 26 > orally 15 > oracles 9) and surface the higher-scored alternative actually in the index (bp-ventures/lightecho-stellar-oracle, 46), with real repo full-names.
mustInclude: Names diadata-org/soroban-oracle-feeders (repoScore 26) as the highest of the three low-scored seed repos.
Surfaces bp-ventures/lightecho-stellar-oracle (repoScore 46, 'medium') as the higher-scored alternative that outranks all three.
mustAvoid: Do not assert one of the low repos is high-quality.
Do not fabricate a score or a repo.
Do not fail to name a real repo full-name.
niceToHave: Orders all three seeds: diadata-org/soroban-oracle-feeders 26 > orally-network/soroban-oracle 15 > diadata-org/soroban-oracles 9.
Notes all three seed repos are SCF-awarded but low-scored.
Describes lightecho-stellar-oracle as a Stellar Soroban Oracle contract by BP Ventures.
passIf: Correctly orders the three seed repos by repoScore (diadata-org/soroban-oracle-feeders 26 > orally-network/soroban-oracle 15 > diadata-org/soroban-oracles 9) OR names soroban-oracle-feeders as the highest of the three, AND surfaces bp-ventures/lightecho-stellar-oracle (46) as the higher-scored alternative.
failIf: Asserts one of the low repos is high-quality.
Fabricates a score/repo.
Cannot name a real repo full-name.
inconclusiveIf: Names soroban-oracle-feeders as the least bad but omits the better-scored lightecho-stellar-oracle alternative.
canonicalFacts: Of the three commonly-surfaced low-score repos, diadata-org/soroban-oracle-feeders has the highest repoScore (26), ahead of orally-network/soroban-oracle (15) and diadata-org/soroban-oracles (9) — all three SCF-awarded but low-scored.
The better-scored alternative in the index is bp-ventures/lightecho-stellar-oracle (repoScore 46, 'medium'), a Stellar Soroban Oracle contract by BP Ventures, which outranks all three.
semantic.discriminator: A guesser cannot rank three near-identical low-score repos or know a better alternative exists; producing the 26>15>9 ordering and surfacing lightecho-stellar-oracle (46) proves a real search_repos query against the repoScore index.

questions.question: Who won the Stellar Hacks: Blend hackathon, and what did the winning projects build?

semantic.intent: Identify all three Stellar Hacks: Blend winners in the CORRECT placement order from the StellarLight hackathon record, with what each project built.
mustInclude: Names all three winners with correct placement: Blend Pool Creator = 1st, Comet x Hoops Finance = 2nd, YieldBack.Cash = 3rd.
mustAvoid: Do not mis-order the placements (e.g. naming the projects but swapping 1st/2nd/3rd).
Do not invent other winners or guess generic DeFi names.
Do not claim there were more than 3 winners.
niceToHave: Notes all three carry the shared 'Blend Composability Award' track.
Notes the event had 39 total submissions and a $6,000 USD prize pool.
Notes it was organized by the Stellar Development Foundation.

Describes what each built: Blend Pool Creator = wizard web app for creating/deploying custom Blend lending pools; Comet x Hoops Finance = Blend Backstop Swap converting USDC<->BLND into the Backstop via Hoops Finance UI; YieldBack.Cash = bond-coupon/yield product on Blend.
passIf: Names all three winners with the correct placement order: Blend Pool Creator 1st, Comet x Hoops Finance 2nd, YieldBack.Cash 3rd.
failIf: Mis-orders the placements while naming the projects.
Invents other winners, swaps placements, or guesses generic DeFi names.
Claims winners other than these three / more than 3 winners.
inconclusiveIf: Names the three projects as winners but is unclear or silent on their 1st/2nd/3rd placement order.
canonicalFacts: Three winners on the single Main Track, all carrying the 'Blend Composability Award': 1st Place — Blend Pool Creator (wizard-style web app for creating/deploying custom Blend lending pools, github.com/ELDEVODE/blend-pool-creator).
2nd Place — Comet x Hoops Finance (a 'Blend Backstop Swap' that converts USDC<->BLND and deposits into the Backstop via the Hoops Finance UI, github.com/hoops-finance/cometswap).
3rd Place — YieldBack.Cash (a bond-coupon / yield product on Blend, github.com/KBenster/yieldback).
The event had 39 total submissions and a $6,000 USD prize pool, organized by the Stellar Development Foundation; these 3 are the only winners (3 of 39 submissions).
semantic.discriminator: A guesser names generic DeFi projects or shuffles the placements; reproducing the exact ordered trio (Blend Pool Creator 1st, Comet x Hoops Finance 2nd, YieldBack.Cash 3rd) out of 39 submissions is strong evidence of a real StellarLight query.

questions.question: Which project took 1st place at Stellar Hacks: KALE x Reflector?

semantic.intent: Identify the actual 1st-place winner of Stellar Hacks: KALE x Reflector from the StellarLight hackathon record, resisting the lure of the obvious KALE-themed names.
mustInclude: Names xbid.ai as the 1st-place winner.
mustAvoid: Do not name Kale Farmers Market (2nd place) or any KALE-themed project as the winner.
Do not say the 1st-place result is unavailable.
niceToHave: Notes 2nd was Kale Farmers Market and 3rd was Not Circle of Trust.
Notes 10 total winners and 46 submissions in the 'Composability' track ($12,000 prize pool).
Describes xbid.ai as a multi-LLM AI agent born on Stellar with real stake and memory of its own outcomes (repo github.com/xbid-ai, dorahacks.io/buidl/32593).
passIf: Answer names xbid.ai (or xbid) as 1st place.
failIf: Names Kale Farmers Market (the 2nd-place entry) or another KALE-themed entry as the winner.
Says the 1st place is not available / unknown.
inconclusiveIf: Names xbid.ai but is unclear whether it was 1st vs another placement.
canonicalFacts: 1st Place at Stellar Hacks: KALE x Reflector went to xbid.ai (dorahacks.io/buidl/32593), a multi-LLM AI agent born on Stellar with real stake and memory of its own outcomes (repo github.com/xbid-ai).
2nd was Kale Farmers Market and 3rd was Not Circle of Trust.
There were 10 total winners and 46 submissions in the 'Composability' track ($12,000 prize pool).
semantic.discriminator: An agent guessing from the obvious KALE-themed names (Kale Farmers Market, etc.) gets it wrong; the actual winner is an AI-agent project whose name does not reference KALE, so a correct answer is strong evidence of a real StellarLight query.

questions.question: How many submissions did Stellar Hacks: KALE x Reflector get compared to Stellar Hacks: Blend, and how many winners did each pay out?

semantic.intent: Compare submission counts and winner counts between Stellar Hacks: KALE x Reflector and Stellar Hacks: Blend using the live get_hackathon stats for both events.
mustInclude: States KALE x Reflector had 46 submissions and Blend had 39 submissions.
States KALE x Reflector paid out 10 winners and Blend paid out 3 winners.
mustAvoid: Do not conflate submissions with winners.
Do not estimate the numbers instead of using the live get_hackathon stats.
Do not get either pair of numbers wrong.
niceToHave: Notes KALE x Reflector had 7 more submissions (46 vs 39).
Notes KALE x Reflector paid out more than triple the winners (10 vs 3).
Notes KALE x Reflector = $12,000 'Composability' track (1st-10th) and Blend = $6,000 'Main Track' (1st-3rd).
passIf: Gives BOTH submission counts correctly (KALE x Reflector = 46, Blend = 39) AND both winner counts (KALE x Reflector = 10, Blend = 3).
failIf: Gets either pair of numbers wrong.
Conflates submissions with winners.
Estimates the numbers rather than sourcing them from the live get_hackathon stats.
inconclusiveIf: Gives one pair correctly (e.g. submissions 46 vs 39) but omits or is vague about the other pair (winner counts).
canonicalFacts: KALE x Reflector had 46 submissions and paid out 10 winners (1st-10th place, $12,000 pool, 'Composability' track).
Stellar Hacks: Blend had 39 submissions and paid out 3 winners (1st-3rd, $6,000 pool, 'Main Track').
KALE x Reflector had 7 more submissions (46 vs 39) and more than triple the number of winners (10 vs 3).
semantic.discriminator: Precise paired counts (46/39 submissions and 10/3 winners) cannot be guessed and require pulling live get_hackathon stats for both events; a guesser conflates submissions with winners or invents round numbers.

questions.question: I'm a Stellar builder looking for an SCF sponsor brief I can actually submit to and get funded for right now. Which RFPs are open this round, and what are they?

answerRegex: contract source verification|oz accounts policy builder

semantic.intent: List the currently-fundable open RFPs (the q2-2026 active-quarter, status=open set) from the StellarLight RFP record, separating them from past closed rounds.
mustInclude: Names the 5 open RFPs: Trustline Onboarder, Passkey UI Kit, Account Demolisher, Contract Source Verification Service, OZ Accounts Policy Builder.
Identifies them as the q2-2026 open / currently-fundable set.
mustAvoid: Do not list any q1-2026 closed RFP (Prices API & Indexing Service, AI-Focused WebIDE, Advanced AMM Framework, Soroban VRF) as open/fundable.
Do not invent RFP titles not in the data.
Do not state a count other than 5 open / 9 closed / 14 total.
niceToHave: Notes categories/authors: Trustline Onboarder (payments, Tyler), Passkey UI Kit (developer-tooling, Keeks), Account Demolisher (consumer-dapps, Jake), Contract Source Verification Service (infrastructure, Matej), OZ Accounts Policy Builder (developer-tooling, Ishan).
Notes the other 9 of the 14 total RFPs are CLOSED (q1-2026) and not currently fundable.
passIf: Answer names the 5 open RFPs (at minimum Trustline Onboarder, Passkey UI Kit, Account Demolisher, Contract Source Verification Service, OZ Accounts Policy Builder) AND identifies them as the q2-2026 open/currently-fundable set.
failIf: Lists any q1-2026 closed RFP (Prices API, AI-Focused WebIDE, Advanced AMM Framework, Soroban VRF, etc.) as open/fundable.
Invents RFP titles not in the data.
States a count other than 5 open / 9 closed / 14 total.
inconclusiveIf: Names some of the 5 open RFPs but is unclear whether they are the q2-2026 open set vs. closed.
canonicalFacts: There are exactly 5 OPEN RFPs, all in active quarter Q2 2026 (q2-2026): Trustline Onboarder (payments, author Tyler), Passkey UI Kit (developer-tooling, Keeks), Account Demolisher (consumer-dapps, Jake), Contract Source Verification Service (infrastructure, Matej), OZ Accounts Policy Builder (developer-tooling, Ishan).
The other 9 of the 14 total RFPs are CLOSED (q1-2026, e.g. Prices API & Indexing Service, AI-Focused WebIDE, Advanced AMM Framework, Soroban VRF) and are NOT currently fundable.
semantic.discriminator: A guesser cannot enumerate the exact 5 open q2-2026 titles or the 5-open/9-closed/14-total split; producing them with correct open-vs-closed status is strong evidence of a real get_rfps query keyed on meta.activeQuarter and status=open.

questions.question: My team works on Soroban smart accounts / passkey auth. Are there any open sponsor briefs in that space I could go after, and how are they different from each other?

semantic.intent: Find the open (q2-2026) RFPs matching the smart-account / passkey theme from the StellarLight RFP record and correctly distinguish them from each other.
mustInclude: Names BOTH open RFPs: Passkey UI Kit and OZ Accounts Policy Builder.
Draws a correct distinction: Passkey UI Kit is a front-end signing/UX UI component library; OZ Accounts Policy Builder is a no-code/low-code policy-configuration/deployment tool.
mustAvoid: Do not name only one of the two.
Do not invent a brief.
Do not present the closed Soroban-First Block Explorer as an open opportunity.
Do not claim there are no matching open RFPs.
niceToHave: Notes Passkey UI Kit (developer-tooling, author Keeks) is a reusable React/React-Native UI component library for passkey registration, authentication, and signing, implementing the OZ smart account standard and WebAuthn spec.

Notes OZ Accounts Policy Builder (developer-tooling, author Ishan) is a no-code/low-code visual editor to compose OpenZeppelin-style smart-account signing policies (multisig thresholds, spending limits, time locks) with Rust/Soroban code export and one-click deploy.
Notes the Soroban-First Block Explorer mentions the OZ smart account standard but is CLOSED / q1-2026, so it is not an open opportunity.
passIf: Answer names BOTH open RFPs — Passkey UI Kit AND OZ Accounts Policy Builder — as the open smart-account/passkey briefs, and draws a correct distinction (UI/signing component library vs. policy-builder/no-code config tool).
failIf: Names only one of the two open RFPs.
Invents a brief.
Presents the closed Soroban-First Block Explorer as an open opportunity.
Claims there are no matching open RFPs.
inconclusiveIf: Names both RFPs but does not draw the UI-component-library vs. policy-builder distinction.
canonicalFacts: Two OPEN (q2-2026) RFPs match smart-accounts/passkeys: Passkey UI Kit (developer-tooling, author Keeks) — a reusable React/React-Native UI component library for passkey registration, authentication, and signing, implementing the OZ smart account standard and WebAuthn spec; and OZ Accounts Policy Builder (developer-tooling, author Ishan) — a no-code/low-code visual editor to compose OpenZeppelin-style smart-account signing policies (multisig thresholds, spending limits, time locks) with Rust/Soroban code export and one-click deploy.
They differ in scope: Passkey UI Kit is a front-end signing/UX component library; OZ Accounts Policy Builder is a policy-configuration/deployment tool.
The Soroban-First Block Explorer also mentions the OZ smart account standard but is CLOSED / q1-2026, so it is not an open opportunity.
semantic.discriminator: A guesser would name a single generic passkey brief or miss that the OZ-mentioning Soroban-First Block Explorer is closed; correctly returning exactly these two open RFPs with their distinct scopes proves a real status-filtered get_rfps query.

questions.question: Was there ever an SCF brief about replacing Kelp / market-making bots on Stellar? What was it called, can I still apply, and which other RFPs were in that same round?

semantic.intent: Identify the Kelp-deprecation / market-making-bot RFP from the StellarLight RFP record, report its closed status (no longer fundable), and place it in its q1-2026 round.
mustInclude: Names the brief as 'Hummingbot Integration (Trading Engine)'.
Identifies it as CLOSED / q1-2026 / no longer fundable.
Ties it to the Kelp-deprecation framing (deprecation of Kelp leaving a liquidity gap).

questions.question: I'm trying to find an underbuilt niche on Stellar. Which project category is the most crowded right now, and which is the most underbuilt — and roughly how many projects are in each?

semantic.intent: Identify the most crowded and most underbuilt Stellar project categories with approximate project counts from the StellarLight clusters endpoint, resisting a generic 'DeFi/payments is crowded' guess.
mustInclude: Names User-Facing App as the largest/most-crowded category with ~204 projects.
Identifies Anchor (1 project) and/or Asset (3 projects) as the most underbuilt with near-zero counts.
mustAvoid: Do not claim generically that 'DeFi/payments is crowded' without category names/counts.
Do not invent categories not in the canonical schema (User-Facing App, Tooling, Infrastructure, Protocol/Contract, Asset, Anchor).
niceToHave: Notes User-Facing App has 110 SCF-funded projects.
Notes Anchor's single project is Bitso and Asset's three are Ondo, RedSwan, Spiko, both with 0 SCF-funded.
Gives the full size ladder: User-Facing App 204 > Tooling 109 > Infrastructure 104 > Protocol/Contract 78 > Asset 3 > Anchor 1.
passIf: Answer names User-Facing App as the largest/most-crowded with ~204 projects AND identifies Anchor (1) and/or Asset (3) as the most underbuilt.
failIf: Claims 'DeFi/payments is crowded' generically without the real category names/counts.
Invents categories not in the canonical schema (User-Facing App, Tooling, Infrastructure, Protocol/Contract, Asset, Anchor).
inconclusiveIf: Names User-Facing App as largest but gives no count and does not identify Anchor or Asset as the underbuilt extreme.
canonicalFacts: Most crowded category: User-Facing App with 204 projects (110 SCF-funded) — by far the largest.
Most underbuilt: Anchor with just 1 project (Bitso) and Asset with 3 projects (Ondo, RedSwan, Spiko), both with 0 SCF-funded.
Size ordering: User-Facing App 204 > Tooling 109 > Infrastructure 104 > Protocol/Contract 78 > Asset 3 > Anchor 1.
The canonical category set is User-Facing App, Tooling, Infrastructure, Protocol/Contract, Asset, Anchor.
semantic.discriminator: A guesser claims 'DeFi/payments is crowded' generically or invents categories; producing the canonical category names with the 204-vs-1/3 counts proves a real query against the StellarLight clusters endpoint.

questions.question: If I'm picking a category to build in based on the best odds of getting SCF funding, which category has the highest SCF-funded ratio — not just the most funded projects in absolute terms?

semantic.intent: Distinguish the highest SCF-funded RATIO category from the highest absolute funded count, naming Protocol/Contract (47/78) as the best-odds ratio while acknowledging User-Facing App leads on absolute funded count (110).
mustInclude: Names Protocol/Contract (47/78, ~60%) as the highest funded-ratio category.
Distinguishes ratio from absolute count and acknowledges User-Facing App leads on absolute funded count (110).
mustAvoid: Do not answer only 'User-Facing App, it has the most funding' — that is the absolute-count trap and ignores the ratio question.
niceToHave: Cites Tooling 45/109 (~41%) and Infrastructure 37/104 (~36%) ratios.
Notes Asset (0/3) and Anchor (0/1) have a 0% funded ratio.
Notes Protocol/Contract is not the largest category yet has the highest ratio.
passIf: Answer names Protocol/Contract (47/78, ~60%) as the highest funded-RATIO category AND acknowledges User-Facing App leads on absolute funded count (110).
failIf: Answers only 'User-Facing App, it has the most funding' without distinguishing ratio from absolute count.
Fabricates per-category funded counts or names a different category as the highest ratio.
inconclusiveIf: Mentions Protocol/Contract but does not give the 47/78 figures or fails to contrast it with User-Facing App's absolute lead.
canonicalFacts: Protocol/Contract has the highest funded ratio: 47 of 78 projects are SCF-funded (~60%), even though it is NOT the largest category.
User-Facing App has the most funded projects in absolute terms (110) but a lower ratio (110/204 ≈ 54%).
Other ratios: Tooling 45/109 (~41%), Infrastructure 37/104 (~36%).
Asset (0/3) and Anchor (0/1) have a 0% funded ratio.
semantic.discriminator: A guesser falls into the absolute-count trap ('User-Facing App has the most funding'); citing the 47/78 ratio and distinguishing it from the 110 absolute lead proves per-category funded counts from a real clusters query that a guesser cannot produce.

questions.question: Tokenized real-world assets are a hot narrative everywhere. On Stellar specifically, is the 'Asset' category a crowded, well-funded lane or an open one — and can you back that with the data?

semantic.intent: Show with cluster data that the Stellar 'Asset' category is open and entirely SCF-unfunded despite the global RWA hype, naming the few real issuers as evidence.
mustInclude: States the Asset category is underbuilt/open with only ~3 projects.
States it has 0 SCF-funded projects ($0 SCF total).
mustAvoid: Do not call RWA/Asset 'hot/crowded' on Stellar or assume it is well-funded — that is the opposite of the grounded answer.
Do not claim the Asset category is large or SCF-backed.
niceToHave: Names the actual projects Ondo, RedSwan, and Spiko.
Notes they are RWA/tokenized-asset issuers (Ondo's USDY, Spiko's tokenized T-bill funds, RedSwan tokenized real estate) that entered without SCF funding.
Frames Asset as one of the smallest, least SCF-supported lanes — an underbuilt opportunity.
passIf: Answer states Asset is underbuilt/open (only ~3 projects) AND has 0 SCF-funded projects ($0 SCF).
failIf: Claims the Asset category is large or SCF-backed.
Calls RWA 'hot/crowded' on Stellar and assumes it is funded, contradicting the data.
inconclusiveIf: Says Asset is small/open but does not state it is 0 SCF-funded / $0 SCF, or vice versa.
canonicalFacts: The Asset category is wide open and entirely unfunded by SCF: only 3 projects (Ondo, RedSwan, Spiko) and 0 of them are SCF-funded ($0 SCF total).
These are notable RWA/tokenized-treasury issuers (Ondo's USDY, Spiko's tokenized T-bill funds, RedSwan tokenized real estate) that exist on Stellar but came in without SCF money.
Despite the global RWA hype, Asset is one of the smallest and least SCF-supported lanes on Stellar — an underbuilt opportunity, not a crowded one.
semantic.discriminator: A guesser working from general crypto knowledge calls RWA 'hot/crowded' and assumes it is funded — the opposite of the grounded answer; stating Asset has only 3 projects and 0 SCF funding is strong evidence of a real StellarLight clusters query.

semantic.intent: Identify the builder in the StellarLight directory who actually WON a Stellar hackathon category (not merely participated), and what they won for, resisting the lure of treating all hackathon-tagged builders as winners.

mustAvoid: Do not present Juan Carlos (Micoh18) or Gabriel Barboza (gabrieIbarboza) as hackathon winners — their bios only say they participated.

Notes a q=hackathon search returns 3 builders total (Pedro, Juan Carlos/Micoh18, Gabriel Barboza) but only Pedro's bio describes actually winning.
Notes Pedro's location is Brazil and roleTitle 'Blockchain Specialist at Nearx'.
passIf: Answer names Pedro Pelicioni Costa (or pedro-pelicioni) AND the specific win: the 'Wonderfully Written' category and/or the project 'The Ultimate Stellar Supreme Tutorial'.
failIf: Returns all 3 hackathon-tagged builders as 'winners' (over-claim).
Presents Juan Carlos/Micoh18 or Gabriel Barboza as hackathon winners.
Names no one / says the directory has no hackathon builders.

canonicalFacts: Pedro Pelicioni Costa (githubUsername pedro-pelicioni, location Brazil, roleTitle 'Blockchain Specialist at Nearx') won the 'Wonderfully Written' category at the Stellar Hackathon (a global event with a $50,000 prize pool) for his tutorial project 'The Ultimate Stellar Supreme Tutorial'.

A q=hackathon search returns 3 builders total — Pedro Pelicioni Costa, Juan Carlos (Micoh18), and Gabriel Barboza (gabrieIbarboza) — but only Pedro's bio describes actually winning; the other two only mention participating in / being passionate about hackathons.

questions.question: Is x402 actually possible on Stellar today, or just theoretical? Can I try it before I build on it?

semantic.intent: Confirm from the StellarLight research/dev-docs layer that x402 is documented and live on Stellar (not theoretical), and that there is a runnable demo, rather than answering from general knowledge of the Coinbase x402 spec.
mustInclude: Answers YES / possible-and-live (not theoretical).
Cites the StellarLight dev-docs 'x402 on Stellar' record or its developers.stellar.org/docs/build/agentic-payments/x402 URL.
mustAvoid: Do not hedge that x402 is merely proposed/unsupported on Stellar.
Do not answer only from general LLM knowledge of the Coinbase x402 spec without surfacing the Stellar-specific dev-docs record.
Do not invent a different source.
niceToHave: Mentions the working x402 Demo runnable on Stellar Testnet or Mainnet.
Notes supported assets are Testnet/Mainnet USDC.
Names a facilitator: Coinbase x402 Facilitator or the Build-on-Stellar Relayer using the OpenZeppelin x402 Facilitator Plugin.
Notes the chunks are high confidence (~0.76) with dev-docs authority ~0.95.
passIf: Answer says x402 is documented and live on Stellar (not theoretical) AND cites the dev-docs 'x402 on Stellar' record or its developers.stellar.org/.../agentic-payments/x402 URL.
failIf: Answers only from general LLM knowledge of the Coinbase x402 spec without surfacing the Stellar-specific dev-docs record.
Hedges that x402 is merely proposed/unsupported on Stellar.
Invents a different source.
inconclusiveIf: Says x402 is live on Stellar but does not cite the dev-docs 'x402 on Stellar' record or its URL.
Names x402 support but is unclear whether a runnable demo / named facilitator exists.
canonicalFacts: x402 is documented and live on Stellar (not theoretical); StellarLight returns the dev-docs page titled 'x402 on Stellar' (developers.stellar.org/docs/build/agentic-payments/x402).
The docs include a working x402 Demo runnable on Stellar Testnet or Mainnet, with supported assets Testnet/Mainnet USDC.
Documented x402 Facilitators include the Coinbase x402 Facilitator and a Build-on-Stellar Relayer using the OpenZeppelin x402 Facilitator Plugin.
The chunks are marked high confidence (~0.76) with dev-docs authority ~0.95.
semantic.discriminator: A guesser answers from the generic Coinbase x402 spec and may hedge that it is unproven on Stellar; a correct answer surfaces the Stellar-specific dev-docs record with a runnable Testnet/Mainnet demo and named facilitators, proving a real StellarLight query.

questions.question: Are there any known reentrancy or authorization-recursion findings in Soroban's core auth from a security audit? How serious was it?

semantic.intent: Surface the StellarLight audit record for the Veridise 'Stellar Soroban Core' authorization-recursion (DoS) finding, report its Critical severity, and capture the nuance that Veridise judged it invalid/not-exploitable rather than presenting it as a live exploitable bug.
mustInclude: Uses the audit source and names the Veridise 'Stellar Soroban Core' audit.
Names the require_auth/check_auth authorization-recursion (DoS) finding.
Reports the severity as Critical.
Notes it was classified invalid/not-exploitable (recursion is finite/linear, costly to attack).
mustAvoid: Do not fabricate a severity.
Do not claim a confirmed exploitable reentrancy hole.
Do not name a wrong auditor.
Do not answer generically about reentrancy without surfacing the StellarLight audit record.
niceToHave: Cites V-SOR-VUL-002 'Denial of Service During Authorization' in rs-soroban-env/src/auth.rs (require_auth_enforcing()).
Cites the report locations sorobansecurity.com/report/28 and the V2.1 report/42.
Explains the recursion: a contract whose check_auth calls require_auth on its own address causes repeated require_auth_enforcing invocations.
Notes the 'Why Invalid' reasoning: a failed require_auth returns an Err that propagates, so iterations are only linear and constructing many trackers is costly.
Notes the related Certora audit of 'Spectra Finance' lists Arbitrary Execution & Reentrancy / 'Confused Deputy' as a covered threat vector.
passIf: Answer names the Veridise 'Stellar Soroban Core' audit and the require_auth/check_auth authorization-recursion (DoS) finding, reports severity Critical, AND notes it was classified invalid/not-exploitable.
failIf: Fabricates a severity.
Claims a confirmed exploitable reentrancy hole.
Names a wrong auditor.
Answers generically about reentrancy without surfacing the StellarLight audit record.
inconclusiveIf: Names the Veridise finding and Critical severity but omits the invalid/not-exploitable nuance.
Surfaces an audit record but is unclear whether the finding is Veridise's Soroban Core auth-recursion DoS.
canonicalFacts: Filtering search_research to source=audit surfaces the Veridise audit of 'Stellar Soroban Core' (sorobansecurity.com/report/28 and the V2.1 report/42).
The finding V-SOR-VUL-002 'Denial of Service During Authorization' in rs-soroban-env/src/auth.rs (require_auth_enforcing()) is flagged at severity CRITICAL.
It concerns a contract whose check_auth calls require_auth on its own address, causing repeated require_auth_enforcing invocations (the frowned-upon recursion).

Veridise marked it 'Why Invalid': a failed require_auth returns an Err that propagates, so iterations are only linear in the number of trackers and constructing many trackers is costly, making the attack not worthwhile — investigated and judged not a real exploit.
A related Certora audit of 'Spectra Finance' also lists Arbitrary Execution & Reentrancy / 'Confused Deputy' as a covered threat vector.
semantic.discriminator: A guesser answers generically about Soroban reentrancy or invents a severity; a correct answer names the specific Veridise 'Stellar Soroban Core' V-SOR-VUL-002 finding as Critical-but-invalid, which only a real StellarLight audit query yields.

questions.question: What SEP defines the standard token interface for Soroban smart contracts, and is it finalized yet?

semantic.intent: Identify SEP-41 'Soroban Token Interface' as the Soroban token standard from the StellarLight sep record and state that its status is Draft (not final/active), resisting the assumption that a widely-used standard is finalized.
mustInclude: Names SEP-41 / 'Soroban Token Interface' as the Soroban token standard.
States its status is Draft (not final/active).
mustAvoid: Do not claim SEP-41 is Final/Active.
Do not cite the wrong SEP number for the Soroban token interface.
Do not answer without retrieving the StellarLight sep record.
niceToHave: Notes it is a subset of the Stellar Asset Contract and compatible with CAP-46-6.
Names an author (e.g., Leigh McCulloch, Jonathan Jove, Siddharth Suresh, or Simon Chow).
Names a dependent SEP: SEP-44 (Soroban Token Interface Extension: Memo) or SEP-56 (Tokenized Vault Standard, requiring SEP-41 compliance).
Notes it returns as the top, highest-confidence record (~0.80, authority 1.0), file sep-0041.md, version 0.4.1, Created 2023-09-22, last Updated 2025-08-28.
passIf: Answer names SEP-41 / 'Soroban Token Interface' as the Soroban token standard AND states its status is Draft (not final/active).
failIf: Claims SEP-41 is Final/Active.
Cites the wrong SEP number for the Soroban token interface.
Answers without retrieving the StellarLight sep record.
inconclusiveIf: Names SEP-41 as the Soroban token interface but does not state its status, or is unclear whether it is Draft vs final.
States there is a Draft Soroban token SEP but does not pin it to SEP-41.
canonicalFacts: SEP-41, titled 'Soroban Token Interface' (sep-0041.md), is returned by search_research (source=sep) as the top, highest-confidence record (~0.80, authority 1.0).
It is NOT finalized — its Status is Draft (Created 2023-09-22, last Updated 2025-08-28, version 0.4.1), authored by Jonathan Jove, Siddharth Suresh, Simon Chow, and Leigh McCulloch.
It defines a standard contract interface for tokens that is a subset of the Stellar Asset Contract and compatible with CAP-46-6.
Other SEPs build on it: SEP-44 (Soroban Token Interface Extension: Memo) and SEP-56 (Tokenized Vault Standard, which requires the underlying asset and the vault to be SEP-41 compliant).
semantic.discriminator: A guesser may name the wrong SEP number or assume a heavily-used token standard is Final; a correct answer pins it to SEP-41 'Soroban Token Interface' AND reports its Draft status, which only the StellarLight sep record provides.
